title: Fama Fraternitatis
subtitle: The Discovery of the Fraternity of the Most Laudable Order of the Rosy Cross — the Rosicrucian Manifesto of 1614

summary.one_line: The foundational Rosicrucian manifesto recounting the life of Christian Rosenkreutz, his Arabian journey, the founding of the Fraternity, and the discovery of his seven-sided vault.
summary.scope: Covers the Fama's address to the wise, the life of C.R.C. from his Cloister youth through Arabian travels to Fez, the founding of the original eight brethren, the six articles of the Order, and the discovery of the Vault with the door inscribed 'Post 120 annos patebo.'

sources.label: Fama Fraternitatis (1614/1615 English edition, transcribed at alchemywebsite.com)

body.text: Wisdom (saith Solomon) is to a man an infinite Treasure, for she is the Breath of the Power of God, and a pure Influence that floweth from the Glory of the Almighty ... with this Treasure was our first Father Adam fully endued: Hence it doth appear, that after God had brought before him all the Creatures of the Field, and the Fowls under Heaven, he gave to every one of them their proper names, according to their nature. Although now through the sorrowful fall into sin this excellent Jewel Wisdom hath been lost, and meer Darkness and Ignorance is come into the World, yet notwithstanding hath the Lord God sometimes hitherto bestowed, and made manifest the same, to some of his Friends.

He became by chance acquainted with the Wise men of Damasco in Arabia, and beheld what great Wonders they wrought, and how Nature was discovered unto them ... he was but of the age of sixteen years when he came thither ... the Wise received him not as a stranger, but as one whom they had long expected, they called him by his name, and shewed him other secrets out of his Cloyster ... At Fez he did get acquaintance with those which are commonly called the Elementary Inhabitants, who revealed unto him many of their secrets ... Of these of Fez he often did confess, that their Magia was not altogether pure, and also that their Cabala was defiled with their Religion; but notwithstanding he knew how to make good use of the same, and found still more better grounds of his Faith, altogether agreeable with the Harmony of the whole World.

Their agreement was this: First, That none of them should profess any other thing, then to cure the sick, and that gratis. 2. None of the Posterity should be constrained to wear one certain kind of habit, but therein to follow the custom of the Country. 3. That every year upon the day C. they should meet together at the house S. Spiritus, or to write the cause of his absence. 4. Every Brother should look out for a worthy person, who after his discease might succeed him. 5. The word C.R. should be their Seal, Mark, and Character. 6. The Fraternity should remain secret one hundred years.

We opened the door, and there appeared to our sight a Vault of seven sides and corners, every side five foor broad, and the height of eight foot; Although the Sun never shined in this Vault, nevertheless it was enlightened with another sun, which had learned this from the Sun, and was scituated in the upper part in the Center of the sieling; in the midst, in stead of a Tomb-stone, was a round Altar covered over with a plate of brass, and thereon this engraven: A.C. R.C. Hoc universi compendium unius mihi sepulchrum feci. Round about the first Circle or Brim stood, Jesus mihi omnia. In the middle were four figures, inclosed in circles, whose circumscription was, 1. Nequaquam vacuum. 2. Legis Jugum. 3. Libertas Evangelij. 4. Dei gloria intacta.

C.R.C.'s journey through Arabia to Fez, where 'the wise received him not as a stranger, but as one whom they had long expected, called him by his name, and shewed him other secrets out of his Cloyster', describes the Steinerian principle of the 'repeated earthly lives' and the 'time spirit' (Zeitgeist). In Steiner's account of cosmic evolution, the Arabian-Persian cultural epoch was the third post-Atlantean period, when the sentient soul developed. The 'Wise men of Damasco' are the guardians of the Hermetic wisdom that Steiner identifies with the original Atlantean mystery schools. The 'Elementary Inhabitants' of Fez are what Steiner calls the 'elemental beings' of nature — the undines, sylphs, gnomes, and salamanders that the initiate learns to perceive and work with. C.R.C.'s statement that 'their Magia was not altogether pure' but that he could 'make good use of the same' corresponds to Steiner's warning that elemental contact must be purified through esoteric Christian development, not pursued for its own sake.

The six articles of the Rosicrucian Fraternity mirror the tantric samaya vows (the 'bond' between guru and disciple). The first article — healing the sick gratis — matches the bodhisattva vow to liberate all beings without expectation of reward. The second — no fixed habit — echoes the mahāsiddha tradition where practitioners wear whatever is customary in their country, concealing their realization. The third — annual meeting — is the tantric gaṇacakra (feast gathering) held on specific sacred days. The fourth — finding a successor — is the lineage transmission (guru-paramparā) where each master initiates a worthy successor. The fifth — the word C.R. as seal — is the mantra-seed syllable (bīja) that carries the lineage's power. The sixth — secrecy for one hundred years — is the tantric commitment to practice in secrecy (guhya) until the realization is stable. In Layayoga, the six articles correspond to the six cakras that must be perfected before the sahasrāra (the seventh) opens.

The seven-sided vault with its 'inner sun' in the center of the ceiling is the human body with its seven cakras, powered by the 'inner sun' of kuṇḍalinī at the crown (sahasrāra). Each side 'five foot broad' — five being the number of mahābhūtas (elements) that constitute each cakra. The height of eight feet — eight being the number of pāśas (bonds) in Tantraloka that must be cut for liberation. The inscriptions on the round altar — 'Nequaquam vacuum' (by no means empty), 'Legis Jugum' (the yoke of law), 'Libertas Evangelij' (freedom of the gospel), 'Dei gloria intacta' (the glory of God untouched) — are the four states of consciousness: waking (vaiśvānara), dreaming (taijasa), deep sleep (prājña), and the fourth (turīya). That 'the Sun never shined in this Vault' yet it was 'enlightened with another sun' is the turīya: the state of illumination that does not depend on external light (sense perception) because it is self-luminous (prakāśa in Kashmiri Shaivism).

The discovery of the vault 'Post 120 annos patebo' after the death of C.R.C. parallels the Tibetan Book of the Dead's description of the third bardo (the search for rebirth). The '120 years' is the maximum period of the intermediate state (the 49 days of the bardo being a compressed symbolic representation of the process). The seven-sided vault is the seven-week bardo journey, where each side is a week of visions. The 'inner sun' that illuminates without external light is the Clear Light of the first bardo, which must be recognized to achieve liberation. The body of C.R.C. 'whole and unconsumed' found beneath the altar is the rainbow body of Dzogchen — the practitioner whose physical form dissolves into light at death rather than decaying, because he has realized the Light Body (the 'Diamond Body' of alchemy). The book 'I' (the 'Book M' of the Brothers) found in his hand containing 'all that which man can desire, wish, or hope for' is the Bardo Thödol — the book that teaches the recognition that liberates in the bardo.

gaps: Full text of C.R.C. journey details from Damascus to Egypt not fully excerpted
The Confessio Fraternitatis (companion document) not yet incorporated
The full description of the vault contents (books, mirrors, lamps) not fully excerpted

issues.title: Confessio Fraternitatis not yet incorporated
issues.description: The Fama is one of three Rosicrucian manifestos. The Confessio and Chymical Wedding are the other two. All three should be linked for a full picture.

issues.title: Historical authenticity of C.R.C.'s journey debated
issues.description: Whether the narrative is purely allegorical, a veiled history of Francis Bacon, or a genuine crypto-history of a secret order is unresolved.

versions.summary: Initial RO covering the Fama Fraternitatis: the wisdom prologue, C.R.C.'s journey, six articles, and the vault discovery.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Fama Fraternitatis source
